abstract: This study examines the current state of digital inclusion and access to information and communication technologies (ICTs) in rural Ecuador, identifying critical barriers and potential pathways for development. Our findings highlight a stark geographical divide in technological infrastructure: only 27% of rural households possess internet access, compared to 67% in urban centers—a disparity further intensified by a shortage of hardware and inconsistent network coverage. While digital literacy initiatives have yielded mixed results, they prove most effective when technical instruction is paired with long-term institutional support. Although public policies like the "Ecuador Digital" strategy have expanded connectivity, they remain hampered by issues of limited reach and financial sustainability. Conversely, NGO interventions have emerged as vital drivers for device distribution and foundational digital training. Despite significant economic constraints and cultural resistance to digital adoption, the proliferation of mobile technology offers a promising avenue for progress. To bridge this digital chasm, we recommend a multi-faceted approach: scaling infrastructure investment, fostering public-private partnerships, creating localized training curricula, and implementing rigorous evaluation frameworks to ensure the long-term efficacy of inclusion policies.
